content: The analysis uses survey-weighted multivariable logistic regression with two pooled NHANES cycles (2011-2014) and 4-year combined weights. Six trace elements (serum iron, blood lead, cadmium, mercury, selenium, manganese) are examined simultaneously as exposures. Cognitive performance is assessed across three distinct neuropsychological domains: immediate and delayed memory (CERAD Word List Learning and Recall), verbal fluency/executive function (Animal Fluency Test), and processing speed/sustained attention (Digit Symbol Substitution Test). Low cognitive performance is operationally defined using the 25th percentile cutoff of each test score distribution — a validated epidemiologic approach that captures clinically meaningful cognitive impairment while maintaining statistical power. Models adjust for age, sex, race/ethnicity, education, poverty income ratio, BMI, smoking status, alcohol use, diabetes history, and hypertension history. Complex survey design is handled using NHANES examination weights (WTMEC2YR divided by 2 for 4-year combination), masked variance PSU (SDMVPSU), and strata (SDMVSTRA).
keywords: Survey-weighted multivariable logistic regression
Two pooled NHANES cycles (2011-2014) with 4-year combined weights
Six trace elements: Fe, Pb, Cd, Hg, Se, Mn
Three cognitive domains: CERAD (memory), AFT (fluency), DSST (processing speed)
Low cognitive performance defined as below 25th percentile
ICP-MS blood metal measurement at CDC laboratories

content: Among 2,002 adults aged 60+ years, serum iron was significantly associated with decreased risk of low cognitive performance across multiple cognitive tests. Blood cadmium was associated with increased odds of low cognitive performance, with the strongest association observed in older adults with comorbid diabetes and hypertension history. Blood lead demonstrated a detrimental association with cognitive function. Blood selenium exhibited a protective effect against cognitive decline. Blood manganese was connected with low cognitive performance. The multi-metal, multi-domain approach reveals that neurotoxic metals (Pb, Cd) consistently harm cognition while essential trace elements (Fe, Se) are protective — a pattern consistent with oxidative stress and neuroinflammation as shared mechanistic pathways.
keywords: Serum iron: protective against low cognitive performance
Blood cadmium: increased odds of low cognitive performance (especially with diabetes/hypertension)
Blood lead: associated with increased odds of cognitive impairment
Blood selenium: protective association against cognitive decline
Blood manganese: connected with low cognitive performance
n=2,002 adults aged 60+ from NHANES 2011-2014

content: The study employs a comprehensive covariate adjustment strategy that addresses the major confounders in metal-cognition research: demographic factors (age, sex, race/ethnicity, education — all strong predictors of cognitive performance), socioeconomic status (poverty income ratio — proxy for nutritional quality and healthcare access), health behaviors (smoking status, alcohol use — both affect metal levels and cognition independently), anthropometrics (BMI — related to metal storage and metabolic health), and chronic disease (diabetes, hypertension — both independently affect cognition and may modify metal neurotoxicity). Subgroup analyses stratified by diabetes and hypertension history go beyond simple adjustment to test for effect modification — revealing that cadmium's neurotoxic effect is amplified in those with existing vascular or metabolic disease burden. This interaction finding has biological plausibility: cadmium-induced oxidative stress compounds diabetes-related vascular damage and blood-brain barrier disruption.
keywords: Comprehensive covariate adjustment: demographics, SES, health behaviors, chronic disease
Subgroup analysis by diabetes status (effect modification)
Subgroup analysis by hypertension status (effect modification)
Cadmium neurotoxicity amplified by diabetes/hypertension comorbidity
Education adjustment critical for cognitive outcome studies
Smoking and alcohol adjustment for metal-independent cognitive effects

content: The study demonstrates that environmental and nutritional trace elements are differentially associated with cognitive function in older U.S. adults at population-relevant exposure levels. The finding that cadmium — a ubiquitous environmental contaminant from industrial emissions, cigarette smoke, and contaminated food — is associated with cognitive impairment even after adjusting for smoking suggests non-tobacco cadmium exposure pathways (dietary, occupational, environmental) contribute to cognitive risk. The protective effect of selenium and iron highlights the importance of adequate nutritional status for brain health in aging populations. The effect modification by diabetes and hypertension has direct clinical relevance: older adults with cardiometabolic comorbidities may be particularly vulnerable to metal-induced neurotoxicity, suggesting that monitoring blood metal levels could be clinically informative in these high-risk subgroups. The cross-sectional design precludes causal inference, and reverse causation (cognitive impairment leading to dietary changes affecting metal levels) cannot be excluded.
keywords: Population-relevant exposure levels of environmental metals
Cadmium cognitive risk beyond tobacco exposure pathway
Selenium and iron as protective nutritional factors for brain health
Cardiometabolic comorbidity amplifies metal neurotoxicity
Clinical relevance for monitoring metals in high-risk elderly
Cross-sectional design limits causal inference

content: Published in the British Journal of Nutrition (IF ~3.3), a Cambridge University Press journal with rigorous peer review. The manuscript follows STROBE guidelines for cross-sectional observational studies. Participant selection from NHANES 2011-2014 is documented with clear inclusion criteria (adults aged 60+ years with complete blood metal and cognitive test data, n=2,002). The complex survey design and 4-year combined weighting procedures are specified. Multiple cognitive domains are assessed using validated, standardized neuropsychological instruments (CERAD, AFT, DSST) administered by trained NHANES examiners following standardized protocols. The study acknowledges limitations including cross-sectional design, potential for residual confounding, and inability to assess temporal relationships. NHANES protocols were approved by the NCHS Ethics Review Board, and all participants provided written informed consent.
keywords: STROBE adherence for cross-sectional study
Published in British Journal of Nutrition (Cambridge University Press)
Participant flow documented (n=2,002 from 2 NHANES cycles)
Validated neuropsychological instruments (CERAD, AFT, DSST)
NHANES Ethics Review Board approved
Complex survey design with appropriate weighting
